title: Sarajevo Film Fest Unveils 51 Films in Competition, Including Sandra Wollner’s ‘Everytime’
full_text: × Skip to main content By Lily Ford Plus Icon Lily Ford @lilyfordtweets Follow Sign Up View All July 23, 2026 4:15am 'Everytime' Courtesy of Sarajevo Film Festival A total of 51 films are in contention for the Heart of Sarajevo Awards at the Bosnian Film Festival. The lineup, released on Thursday, spans across four competition selections: narrative feature film, feature and short documentary film, narrative short film and student short film. From these, 21 are to have their world premiere at the August event. One will screen out of competition. “At a time when films are more accessible than ever, the value of a film festival lies not in making films available, but in returning them to public life,” said Izeta Građević, creative director of the Sarajevo Film Festival , whose selection team saw 1,215 films ahead of the fest. Related Stories Movies Venice Unveils 2026 Lineup (Updating Live) Movies 'Brave New Love' Dives Into Lust, Infidelity, Norms, Motherhood and Shame (Exclusive Clip) “Over more than three decades, the Sarajevo Film Festival has earned the trust of an audience willing to embrace films it might never have chosen on its own. This is an audience that does not come to the cinema seeking confirmation of its own taste, but for the excitement of discovering a different perspective on the world,” she continued. “It is for this audience that we selected this year’s programs. They reflect the vitality and diversity of contemporary Southeast European cinema. Each film speaks in its own distinct cinematic voice, yet they all share a search for a different perspective on reality.” Director of the European Film Academy Matthijs Wouter Knol and Yorgos Lanthimos’ producer Athina Rachel Tsangari are among the industry professionals tapped to join British star Emily Watson on the Sarajevo Film Festival jury. Watson will also receive the Honorary Heart of Sarajevo, alongside Iranian filmmaker Asghar Farhadi . Check out the full lineup below ahead of the 32nd Sarajevo Film Festival, running Aug. 14-21. title: TelevisaUnivision Posts Q2 Loss Despite Boost From Mexico World Cup Telecasts
full_text: Jul 23, 2026 4:00am PT TelevisaUnivision Posts Q2 Loss Despite Boost From Mexico World Cup Telecasts By Brian Steinberg Plus Icon Brian Steinberg Senior TV Editor bristei Latest Comcast Q2 Boosted by World Cup, Profits at Peacock 35 minutes ago World Cup May Be Over, but Fox Sports CEO Eric Shanks Thinks Hydration Breaks Could Return 20 hours ago Zoom, With New Story, Bets on Indie Journalist to Tell It 2 days ago See All Getty Images Spanish-language media giant TelevisaUnivision said it posted a loss in the second quarter despite a swell of revenue from its rights to air the World Cup in Mexico, a sign of the challenges the company, formed from the combination of Univision in the U.S. and Grupo Televisa in Mexico, continues to face. The company posted a loss of more than $10 million on revenue of nearly $1.33 billion. Revenue for the period soared 10%, compared with $1.21 billion in the year-earlier period. TelevisaUnivision attributed much of the increase to advertising and licensing fees associated with its Mexican sports rights. Related Stories Šimon Holý Turns Drag, Family and Small-Town Life Into a Tender Political Statement in ‘Chica Checa’ 'Black Money for White Nights' Review: A Cycle of Corruption Endangers a Marriage in a Bitter, Gripping Bulgarian Tragicomedy Indeed, advertising from operations in Mexico rose 19%, driven in large part by commercials tied to telecasts of FIFA World Cup , compared with lackluster results in the U.S,. where advertising revenue slumped 22%. Overall ad revenue fell 9% during the period. TelevisaUnivision in April replaced its ad-sales chief , just days before holding its annual “upfront” presentation. Popular on Variety Subscription and licensing revenue rose 40% during the second quarter, to $621 million. Approximately $90 million in revenue came from sublicensing the company’s FIFA World Cup rights to media properties in other Spanish-speaking Latin American countries. TelevisaUnivision has been working to bolster its balance sheet since Wade Davis, the former Viacom CFO who orchestrated a buyout of Univision in 2020, ceded his CEO role to Daniel Alegre, a former senior executive at Activision Blizzard. Since Alegre joined in 2024, TelevisaUnivision has worked to streamline operations that had previously been siloed by geographic region. In a statement, Alegre gave credit to the power of the soccer spectacle. “The tournament reinforced why soccer is a cornerstone of our strategy and why we are the ‘Home of Soccer’ for Hispanics. Sports, together with our continued investments in live events and premium entertainment, ensure that we remain the destination for culturally relevant moments that matter,” the executive said. “Combined with our unique connection to our audience and the importance of our platforms heading into the U.S. election cycle, this momentum positions us well to drive sustained profitability while creating even greater value for our audiences, advertisers, and partners.” Operating expenses increased 16% to $940 million, mainly driven by sports costs associated with the FIFA World Cup. Operating income was $224 million, a decrease of $17 million versus prior year, which the company attributed to higher direct operating expenses. title: Michelle Zhou’s Venice Horizons Title ‘Big Little Things’ Boarded By Moebius Entertainment For Sales
full_text: Skip to main content Dismiss News Banner Breaking News Venice Film Festival Lineup Announcement — Follow Live Read the full story By Liz Shackleton Liz Shackleton pmc-media-editor-2024 More Stories By Liz Edko Films Picks Up World Rights To Yeung Leung Chuen’s Toronto Discovery Title ‘We Are Born Good’ Moebius Entertainment Picks Up World Rights To Fiona Roan Feng-I’s ‘Happily Ever After’ Ahead of Toronto Premiere Na Hong-Jin’s Cannes Competition Title ‘Hope’ Has Strong Opening At Korean Box Office View All July 23, 2026 3:56am Big Little Things Moebius Entertainment Hong Kong-based Moebius Entertainment has boarded international sales for Michelle Zhou’s Big Little Things , which has been selected to premiere in the Horizons section of this year’s Venice Film Festival. The film follows a woman who returns to her hometown of Harbin after years living in the United States and finds herself caught between two realities: the life she has built with her girlfriend, and the expectations of her family and community. Produced as a Hong Kong-US collaboration, the film explores identity, family, cultural displacement, and the difficult choices faced by those navigating between tradition and self-determination. Related Stories News Venice Film Festival Lineup: Casey Affleck, Werner Herzog, Hirokazu Koreeda, Lee Chang-Dong & Martin McDonagh In Competition News Venice Biennale President Slams EU For Pulling Funding Over Russia's Presence At Art Biennale At Film Festival Conference “ Big Little Things represents the arrival of a distinctive new filmmaking voice,” said Fred Tsui, founder and CEO of Moebius Entertainment. “Michelle Zhou captures the quiet but profound conflicts that define many lives today – the tension between personal truth and the roles we inherit. Her film is deeply intimate yet universally resonant, and we believe audiences around the world will connect with the heroine’s journey.” Watch on Deadline The film was previously presented with the Activator Market Potential Award at the 2025 Golden Horse Film Project Promotion (FPP) in Taipei, where it was selected among the year’s most promising projects from emerging Asian filmmakers. Moebius Entertainment is handling festival representation and worldwide sales rights. The company also recently picked up Taiwanese filmmaker Fiona Roan Feng-I’s Happily Ever After , which will make its world premiere in the Special Presentations section of the Toronto International Film Festival (TIFF). title: Venice Film Festival Lineup: Casey Affleck, Werner Herzog, Hirokazu Koreeda, Lee Chang-Dong & Martin McDonagh In Competition
full_text: Skip to main content Dismiss News Banner Breaking News Venice Film Festival Lineup Announcement — Follow Live Read the full story By Zac Ntim Zac Ntim International Reporter @ZacharyNtim More Stories By Zac Isabel Davis Appointed Director Of The BFI Filmmaking Fund Scottish Ballet & Forest Of Black Partner With Tull Stories To Release Dance Film ‘These Violent Delights’ — First Look Trailer TIFF 2026: ‘Stuffed’ Starring Jodie Comer & Harry Melling Among Discovery Titles View All July 23, 2026 3:52am Jack O'Connell In 'Ink' Danny Boyle Alberto Barbera presided over a lengthy press conference Thursday morning, during which he announced the stacked lineup for this year’s Venice Film Festival, which runs from 2-12 September. Scroll down for the full list of titles. The lineup is expansive, with several big names returning to the festival across its many strands. However, there are notably no big studios titles in competition. In competition, Casey Affleck will debut Company , his latest film as a director. Previously described as a “gothic horror”, the film stars Nick Nolte ( Cape Fear ), Adelaide Clemens ( The Great Gatsby ), Ben Mendelsohn ( The Dark Knight Rises ), Scoot McNairy ( A Complete Unknown ), Emily Alyn Lind ( We Were Liars ) and Caylee Cowan. Related Stories News Venice Giornate Degli Autori Unveils 2026 Selection Dedicated To Late Founder Giorgio Gosetti Festivals Venice Critics' Week Unveils 2026 Selection With Films From Colombia, China & Tunisia South Korean filmmaker Lee Chang-Dong returns to feature filmmaking with Possible Love, his first film since Burning (2018). Japanese filmmaker Hirokazu Koreeda lands at Venice with Look Back , his second festival title of 2026. The filmmaker was also at Cannes in May with Sheep in the Box. Martin McDonagh returns to Venice with Wild Horse Nine , his first feature since The Banshees of Inisherin , which won Best Screenplay and Actor at Venice in 2022 before landing 9 Oscar noms. Veteran Italian filmmaker Nanni Moretti returns to Venice with a film for the first time since 1989. The feature is Succederà Questa Notte and stars Louis Garrel. Watch on Deadline Out of Competition, Paul Schrader will screen a new project titled The Basics Of Philosophy starring Jack Huston and Sofia Boutella. The film is said to follow a repressed philosophy professor who has been grappling with lingering guilt over a decision from his past when the victim suddenly returns to his life. Venice regular Luca Guadagnino will be on the Lido this year but not with his Open AI feature Artificial . The Italian filmmaker will instead screen his long-gestating Bernardo Bertolucci documentary Joie De Vivre . The film runs seven hours long. Wim Wenders is also out of competition with a doc on the Swiss architect Peter Zumthor. The festival has already announced that this year’s opening title will be Danny Boyle’s Rupert Murdoch biopic Ink. The film, starring Jack O’Connell, Guy Pearce and Claire Foy, will screen on September 2 in the Sala Grande at the Palazzo del Cinema on the Lido. Boyle directs the feature from a screenplay written by James Graham (Dear England), based on his Tony-nominated play Ink . O’Connell stars as The Sun newspaper editor Larry Lamb with Pearce as Rupert Murdoch. Claire Foy is Jules Davies. Announcing the title earlier this summer, Barbera described the title as an “account of publisher Rupert Murdoch’s acquisition of the daily newspaper The Sun, which he entrusted to the unscrupulous Larry Lamb, turning it into Britain’s best-selling tabloid at the expense of its rival, The Mirror. ” The festival’s closing title will be Dio ride , written and directed by Giovanni Veronesi ( The King’s Musketeers ). The film will screen out of competition. Pierfrancesco Favino, Silvio Orlando, Alma Noce, Francesco Gheghi, Maurizio Lombardi, Paolo Rossi and Carlo Cecchi star in the film. George Clooney and Ellen Burstyn will be the recipients of the Venice Film Festival ’s Golden Lion for lifetime achievement this year. Maggie Gyllenhaal will serve as the competition jury president. She will be joined by Kaouther Ben Hania, Daniel Blumberg, Francesco Casetti, Xavier Giannoli, Shahrbanoo Sadat, and Johnnie To. The festival will run from September 2 – 12. title: Venice Title ‘The Difficult Bride’ Taps Films Boutique for World Sales (EXCLUSIVE)
full_text: Jul 23, 2026 3:48am PT Venice Title ‘The Difficult Bride’ Taps Films Boutique for World Sales (EXCLUSIVE) By Naman Ramachandran Plus Icon Naman Ramachandran Latest Whoopi Goldberg Slams Transphobic Critics of Elliot Page’s Casting in ‘The Odyssey’: ‘Then Don’t Go See It… Why Do You Care Who’s Cast in It?’ 41 minutes ago Daniel Wu on ‘Cold War 1994,’ ‘Twilight of the Warriors’ and Coming Home to Hong Kong: ‘The Craft Still Challenges Me’ 2 hours ago Yeon Sang-ho Returns to ‘Parasyte’ Universe With ‘Tamiya,’ Starring Shioli Kutsuna, at Netflix 12 hours ago See All Films Boutique Films Boutique has boarded international sales on “ The Difficult Bride ,” Rubaiyat Hossain ‘s supernatural feminist drama selected for the Horizons competition of the Venice Film Festival , where it will become the first Bangladeshi feature film to screen. The cast includes three Bangladesh National Film Award winners – Azmeri Haque Badhon, Rikita Nondine Shimu and Sunerah Kamal – alongside Zaineen Chowdhury Karim in her lead acting debut and Dameer Khan in a guest appearance. Related Stories Jeffrey Wright Says Being Black Is a 'Political Term,' Talks Racist Abuse Against Kylian Mbappé: ‘They Think They Can Diminish How Powerful He Is With Their Stupidity' 'Robert Richardson: The White Devil' Review: Candid Documentary Turns the Camera on a Cinematography Legend “The Difficult Bride” unfolds at Mayfair, a longtime Dhaka beauty parlor, and follows Zaineen Chowdhury Karim’s character as her wedding preparations draw her into an unsettling world shaped by beauty, faith, memory and fear. The screenplay draws on an urban legend Hossain heard as a child, about a bride who reportedly died at the salon under mysterious circumstances. Popular on Variety Principal photography was in Bangladesh, with post-production completed in Paris and Lisbon. The film is Hossain’s fourth feature and received post-production support from the inaugural Fondazione Prada Film Fund. Hossain’s debut feature, “Meherjaan” (2011), picked up awards at the Northampton and Tiburon festivals. Her second feature, “Under Construction” (2015), screened at Locarno and Stockholm. Her third feature, “Made in Bangladesh” (2019), premiered in Toronto’s Contemporary World Cinema section, also screened at Locarno and BFI London, and brought her a first Asia Pacific Screen Award nomination. Jean-Christophe Simon, CEO of Films Boutique, said: “We’re particularly proud to be trusted with Rubaiyat’s new film. Following the success of ‘Made in Bangladesh,’ she has created yet another genuinely unique cinematic work, that will allow for an uncompromising female character to reach audiences worldwide. Both from an esthetic and topical standpoint, her powerful and particularly entertaining movie will speak to many about the situations so many women are still confronted with, when it comes to the way patriarchal society looks at their body.” “The Difficult Bride” is produced by France’s Les Films de l’Après-Midi, in co-production with Portugal’s Midas Filmes, Bangladesh’s Khona Talkies, Norway’s Barentsfilm and Germany’s Tandem Production. The film’s producers are François d’Artemare, Pedro Borges, Rubaiyat Hossain, Aadnan Imtiaz Ahmed, Ingrid Lill Høtgun and Anna Katchko. Md Shariful Islam and Kevin Chaty serve as executive producers. The film also received backing from Eurimages, France’s CNC, Portugal’s ICA, Norway’s Sorfønd and Germany’s World Cinema Fund. The film was shot by cinematographer Leonor Teles, who claimed the Berlin Film Festival’s Golden Bear for a short film in 2016 at a younger age than any prior winner. British composer Jim Williams, whose credits include Julia Ducournau’s “Raw” and “Titane,” wrote the score. Hossain said: “A film is born from the long years of work, sacrifice, patience, creativity and love of many people. I am deeply grateful to our entire team, our actors, crew members, co-producers and collaborators.” In 2010, Ishtiaque Zico’s short film “720 Degrees,” produced by Khona Talkies, became the first Bangladeshi short to screen in Horizons. The selection of “The Difficult Bride” comes as women’s rights in Bangladesh face pressure from extremist religious groups. The festival runs Sept. 2-12. title: Alex Gibney’s Elon Musk Documentary to Premiere in Venice — With a Nearly 4-Hour Runtime
full_text: × Skip to main content By Patrick Brzeski Plus Icon Patrick Brzeski Asia Bureau Chief View All July 23, 2026 3:46am Elon Musk in a fateful appearance at The New York Times Dealbook Summit 2023 at Jazz at Lincoln Center on November 29, 2023 in New York City Photo by Slaven Vlasic/Getty Images for The New York Times Alex Gibney ‘s long-gestating Elon Musk documentary is finally ready for showtime. Musk , the Oscar winner’s years-in-the-making portrait of the controversial technocrat cum oligarch, will premiere at the Venice Film Festival in September — and it arrives at a scale perhaps befitting the world’s first trillionaire: 232 minutes, just shy of four hours. Venice artistic director Alberto Barbera revealed the selection in Rome Thursday while unveiling the festival’s 2026 lineup, and he paused to make sure the audience appreciated just how much Elon has been packed into Gibney’s examination. “I invite you to look at the duration of the movie — four hours,” Barbera said with a wry smile. Related Stories Movies Venice Unveils 2026 Lineup (Updating Live) Movies 'Brave New Love' Dives Into Lust, Infidelity, Norms, Motherhood and Shame (Exclusive Clip) “[Gibney] traces the whole career — the contradictions, and the conditioning power that is centralized in the hands of a single man,” the festival chief added. “Consider a genius of capitalist entrepreneurship, but also a dangerous technocrat, with no moral scruples.” Few recent documentaries of such high profile have had such a long and tricky trip to screen. Gibney first revealed he was at work on the film in March 2023, billing it as a “definitive and unvarnished examination” of the Tesla, SpaceX and X chief. The director reportedly delivered a finished cut in early 2025 — only to return to the editing suite amid Musk’s vocal support and financial backing of Donald Trump’s presidential campaign, followed by his stint next door to the Oval Office as the de facto head of the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE). As Musk continued generating controversial headlines, those following Gibney’s project naturally began to wonder when and how the director would ever be able to wrap up the movie. Last summer, the film’s backers, HBO Documentary Films and Bleecker Street, acknowledged their director’s predicament, putting out a joint statement, saying: “The film continues to grow in scope as the story evolves.” And there was still more to come… Last month, SpaceX’s record-shattering IPO — the largest public offering in history, raising some $75 billion at a roughly $1.8 trillion valuation — lifted Musk’s fortune to the mind-bogglingly obscene point that he is now regarded as the world’s first trillionaire. Musk has been characteristically unrestrained in his reaction to the documentary. “It’s a hit piece,” he posted on X when the project was first reported in 2023, prompting Gibney to reply: “How would you know?” Musk also once tweeted — “Gibney is a douche” — to which, Gibney has said he responded: “douché.” Bleecker Street will release Musk in U.S. theaters on Oct. 16, with HBO Documentary Films holding North American television and streaming rights and Black Bear handling international sales. Gibney’s Jigsaw Productions produced with Zhang Xin’s Closer Media, Double Agent and Anonymous Content. Gibney has spent the past three decades of his career critiquing powerful institutions and the men who run them. He won the best documentary feature Oscar for 2007’s Taxi to the Dark Side , was nominated for Enron: The Smartest Guys in the Room and won widespread critical acclaim for Going Clear: Scientology and the Prison of Belief . He’s tackled Silicon Valley icons and villains before, too, with projects like Steve Jobs: The Man in the Machine and the Elizabeth Holmes exposé The Inventor: Out for Blood in Silicon Valley . He is no stranger to the Lido, having premiered his Mikhail Khodorkovsky portrait Citizen K there in 2019. The 83rd Venice Film Festival runs Sept. 2-12. THR

title: Isabel Davis Named Director of BFI Filmmaking Fund – Global Bulletin
full_text: Jul 23, 2026 3:43am PT Isabel Davis Named Director of BFI Filmmaking Fund – Global Bulletin By Naman Ramachandran Plus Icon Naman Ramachandran Latest Whoopi Goldberg Slams Transphobic Critics of Elliot Page’s Casting in ‘The Odyssey’: ‘Then Don’t Go See It… Why Do You Care Who’s Cast in It?’ 39 minutes ago Yeon Sang-ho Returns to ‘Parasyte’ Universe With ‘Tamiya,’ Starring Shioli Kutsuna, at Netflix 12 hours ago Toronto-Bound Hong Kong Drama ‘We Are Born Good’ Boarded by Edko Films for World Sales 20 hours ago See All BFI LOTTERY LEADER Isabel Davis has been appointed director of the BFI National Lottery Filmmaking Funds , overseeing some £20 million ($26.8 million) a year in support for U.K. independent filmmakers. Davis joins the British Film Institute from Screen Scotland , where she has served as executive director for eight years, and begins a five-year fixed-term contract in October. She succeeds Mia Bays , the outgoing director who has run the fund since 2021 and overseen BAFTA wins for “Kneecap” and “My Father’s Shadow” for outstanding debut in consecutive years, along with a best British short animation win for “Two Black Boys in Paradise.” Before Screen Scotland, Davis spent seven years as the BFI’s head of international, helping broker the organization’s co-production treaties with China and Brazil. Related Stories Kevin Bacon, Kyra Sedgwick and Travis Bacon Talk About Their Bloody ‘Family Movie’ – and Why ‘Violence Can Be Hysterical’: ‘We Are Really Twisted People’ Jesse Eisenberg on Why It Would Be ‘Silly’ to Leave U.S. Because of Trump, Says ‘The Debut’ Is ‘The Opposite of AI’ Following A24 DeepMind Partnership “I’m honored to be stepping into this role,” Davis said, at a moment she called critical for the future of U.K. film. Ben Roberts , BFI chief executive, called her “tenacious when it comes to doing the right thing for U.K. independent film.” Popular on Variety CASTING CALL The BBC has cast Toheeb Jimoh and Dujonna Gift in the lead roles of “Open Water,” its eight-part adaptation of Caleb Azumah Nelson ‘s debut novel, with filming underway in Birmingham and London. Mam Tor Productions, a Banijay U.K. company, and B-Side Productions are making the series for BBC iPlayer and BBC One. Jimoh, known for “Ted Lasso” and “Industry,” plays Marcus, a retail worker developing a career as a photographer; Gift, making her lead TV debut, plays Effie, a dance student in Dublin. They are joined by a cast including Rhianna Dorris, Nadine Marshall and Sherise Blackman. “This is a dream project for me,” Jimoh said. title: Tom Holland Is Filming Fred Astaire Biopic Next and Plans to ‘Do All of the Dancing’ Himself: ‘I’ve Got So Much Work to Do’
full_text: Jul 23, 2026 3:42am PT Tom Holland Is Filming Fred Astaire Biopic Next and Plans to ‘Do All of the Dancing’ Himself: ‘I’ve Got So Much Work to Do’ By K.J. Yossman Plus Icon K.J. Yossman @KJYossman Latest Top Trumps Panel Show, New Seasons of ‘Mock the Week’ Unveiled as Part of Warner Bros. Discovery U.K.’s TLC Comedy Slate 1 day ago Lisa Nandy Stays in Post as U.K. Culture Secretary, Kanishka Narayan Appointed First AI Minister 2 days ago World Cup Final Ratings Skyrocket Across Europe, Despite England and France Losses 3 days ago See All Getty/Everett Tom Holland is on the second of two consecutive blockbuster press tours in the space of a month – first for “The Odyssey” and now for “Spider-Man: Brand New Day” – but the actor is already gearing up for his next role: Fred Astaire . Holland, who is set to play the debonair dancer in an upcoming biopic, revealed he’s already started rehearsing for the film, which was first announced five years ago . Related Stories Dustin Hoffman Says America Is as 'Divided' as During Vietnam War ... and Jokes He Is Still Trying to Figure Out Who He Is Karlovy Vary Focuses on Next Generation of European Filmmakers With Future Frames Program “As soon as I finish with these tours, I’m diving back into the dance studio,” he told “Good Morning America’s” George Stephanopoulos. “I did my first few rehearsals recently, and it both filled me with excitement and absolute dread because I’ve got so much work to do to to try and do Fred proud.” Popular on Variety Per Holland, that means “no doubles, to do all of the dancing [myself], to shoot those dances in one shot — how he would have done it.” In an interview the following day on “Live With Kelly and Mark,” Holland expanded on the project, saying that having “The Odyssey” and “Brand New Day” come out within weeks of each other felt like he had closed the chapter on the first act in her career and is now ready to move into more mature roles. “And what I’m looking for in this next chapter is like the ultimate challenge, and to find things that really scare me,” he explained. “I did my first dance rehearsal the other day for ‘Fred,’ and I was really excited afterwards, but I was equally terrified because I have so much work to do.” Holland is no stranger to an arabesque, having famously got his big break in “Billy Elliott the Musical” in the West End, which required copious dancing. But the actor admitted that one of his biggest regrets is that “after I finished ‘Billy Elliot,’ I quit dancing.” Taking on the role of Astaire has given him an opportunity to step into a pair of dance shoes again and Holland revealed the project had even seen him return to the central London dance studio in which he’d first rehearsed for “Billy Elliott.” Said Holland: “I’m so excited for the challenge.” Travis Scott’s Hollywood Odyssey: The Rapper on Acting for Christopher Nolan, His New Paramount Deal and Never Winning a Grammy Shaken, Soaked and Seasick: What It’s Like to Survive ‘The Odyssey’ in 4DX Loading comments... title: Venice Biennale President Slams EU For Pulling Funding Over Russia’s Presence At Art Biennale At Film Festival Conference
full_text: Skip to main content Dismiss News Banner Breaking News Venice Film Festival Lineup Announcement — Follow Live Read the full story By Melanie Goodfellow Melanie Goodfellow Senior International Film Correspondent @mel_goodfellow More Stories By Melanie Vertical Takes North America For SXSW Thriller ‘The Saviors’ With Adam Scott & Danielle Deadwyler Charles Gaines Dies: ‘Pumping Iron’ Author Who Helped Launch Arnold Schwarzenegger Was 84 Venice Giornate Degli Autori Unveils 2026 Selection Dedicated To Late Founder Giorgio Gosetti View All July 23, 2026 3:37am Pietrangelo Buttafuoco Getty Images Venice Biennale Foundation President Pietrangelo Buttafuoco hit out at the European Commission on Thursday for its move to withdraw funding over Russia’s participation in its Art Biennale earlier this year. Speaking in opening remarks of the press conference unveiling the lineup for 83rd edition of the Venice Film Festival, Buttafuoco suggested the European Commission was “abandoning” the Biennale and its different chapters. “They are abandoning us today because of a political decision, certainly not a legal decision, and certainly not a cultural decision. They are abandoning us in our journey, but La Biennale cannot accept that,” he said. Watch on Deadline Related Stories News Venice Film Festival Lineup Announcement -- Follow Live News Venice Giornate Degli Autori Unveils 2026 Selection Dedicated To Late Founder Giorgio Gosetti His comments follow an announcement earlier this week by the European Commission’s Education and Culture Executive Agency (EACEA) that it was following through with a threat to end €2M ($2.3M) grant in response to the Biennale’s invite to Russia to participate in the Art Biennale. The Biennale responded in a statement that it would lodge an appeal, noting that the withdrawn funds were not used for the Art Biennale but rather the activities of its Cinema Department, and in particular its Biennale College and the Venice Production Bridge initiatives. The stand-off began with the opening of the Art Biennale in April, which was marked by protests over Russia’s return and the resignation of the international jury. The European Union’s head of foreign policy Kaja Kallas said at the time that the invite to Russia violated EU sanctions imposed on Russia in the wake of its invasion of Ukraine in February 2022 and the subsequent ongoing war. Continuing his rebuttal at the film festival press conference, Buttafuoco said bowing to the EU’s demands would fly in the face of the Biennale’s principles. “We would renounce our principles of freedom of expression, of research, fantasy, imagination, values that for 131 years have been characterized this institution,” he said. Referring to the fable of The Wolf & the Lamb, Buttafuoco detailed three letters the European Commission had sent to the Biennale, suggesting the European Union was tantamount to the wolf, and the Cinema Department to the lamb. “In the third letter, the wolf simply eats the lamb,” he said. title: ‘Oasis,’ Exploring Fear, Exclusion and Violence, Picked Up by Totem Ahead of Venice Premiere (EXCLUSIVE)
full_text: Jul 23, 2026 3:25am PT ‘Oasis,’ Exploring Fear, Exclusion and Violence, Picked Up by Totem Ahead of Venice Premiere (EXCLUSIVE) By Leo Barraclough Plus Icon Leo Barraclough International Features Editor LeoBarraclough Latest Sarajevo Film Festival Unveils Competition Programs With 21 World Premieres, Six International Premieres 2 hours ago Square Eyes Acquires ‘I Plant My Hand in the Garden’ Ahead of World Premiere in Venice Days (EXCLUSIVE) 19 hours ago Emily Watson Joined on Sarajevo Film Festival Jury by Athina Rachel Tsangari, Igor Bezinović and More 22 hours ago See All Courtesy of Zeitgeist Filmproduktion FFM Totem has picked up international sales rights for “Oasis,” by writer-director Jannis Lenz , which has its world premiere in the Horizons competition section at the Venice Film Festival . The film joins Totem’s sales slate as the company shifts its primary focus to production. The film centers on a school where a viral video of a student threatening his classmates surfaces online, and the situation spirals into escalating violence. Crushed by the pressure of teachers and parents, three outcasts take refuge in a nearby forest. There, play hardens into ritual and pain becomes an escape, until old wounds surface and the boundaries between them are redrawn. Related Stories How Filmmaker Tonia Mishiali Tackles Immigration, Patriarchy and Women’s Resilience in ’The Lion at My Back’ Switzerland Shows Its Dark Side in Jan-Eric Mack's ‘A Happy Family’: ‘People Don’t Talk’ “ ‘Oasis’ captures the fragility of adolescence with great precision, exploring fear, exclusion and violence through a deeply human perspective,” producer Markus Katsch of Zeitgeist said. Popular on Variety Bérénice Vincent, CEO at Totem, commented: “Jannis is a hugely promising talent, with a rare assurance behind the camera. What impressed us is the way he exposes the contradiction between the violence we’ve normalized and the violence we condemn – and the world as it stands proves his point every day.” Lenz’s previous work includes the short “Wannabe” (2017), which won the Prix Clermont-Ferrand and was nominated for the European Film Award, while his graduation film “Soldat Ahmet” (2021) premiered at Visions du Réel and won several international awards. He is a Vienna Film Academy graduate, mentored by Jessica Hausner, has been an assistant to Michael Haneke, and is a Berlinale Talents alumnus. “Oasis” features newcomer Florian Geißelmann (“Sound of Falling”) and acclaimed actor Godehard Giese (“Rose”), as well as upcoming German actors Anton Petzold and Mathilda Smith. The film is produced by Germany’s Zeitgeist Filmproduktion FFM and co-produced by Austria’s Wega Film and Germany’s Third Picture in collaboration with We Fade to Grey, Ocean Pictures Filmproduktion and Totem Films. It is supported by Der Beauftragte der Bundesregierung für Kultur und Medien BKM, Deutscher Filmförderfonds DFFF, Österreichisches Filminstitut ÖFI+, MFG Medien- und Filmgesellschaft Baden-Württemberg, Film- und Medienstiftung NRW, Kuratorium junger deutscher Film and Filmförderungsanstalt FFA. title: Comcast Q2 Boosted by World Cup, Peacock Swings to First Quarterly Profit
full_text: Jul 23, 2026 3:25am PT Comcast Q2 Boosted by World Cup, Peacock Swings to First Quarterly Profit By Brian Steinberg Plus Icon Brian Steinberg Senior TV Editor bristei Latest TelevisaUnivision Posts Q2 Loss Despite Boost From Mexico World Cup Telecasts 11 minutes ago Comcast Q2 Boosted by World Cup, Peacock Swings to First Quarterly Profit 46 minutes ago World Cup May Be Over, but Fox Sports CEO Eric Shanks Thinks Hydration Breaks Could Return 20 hours ago See All Courtesy of Comcast Comcast said its second quarter was boosted by revenue tied to World Cup telecasts on the company’s Spanish-language Telemundo network as well as the Peacock streaming service, which achieved profitability for the first time, but the company still faced headwinds in its cable and broadband business and saw profit in the period decline due to comparisons with the year-earlier period, which included a sizable gain tied to the sale of its stake in Hulu to Walt Disney. Related Stories Karim Kassem Broaches Migrant Issues in Lebanon With ‘Pipes’: ‘A Lot of People Are Being Affected by War but We Don’t See the Whole Picture’ ‘An Icon Is Here’: Juliette Binoche Takes Over Karlovy Vary, Talks Directorial Debut ‘In-I in Motion’ and Explains Why ‘We’re All Creators’ The Philadelphia owner of NBC and the Universal movie studio posted net income of $3.5 billion, or 99 cents per share, compared with $2.98 per share in the year-earlier period, or $11.1 billion. Comcast gained $9.4 billion from the sale of its interest in Hulu. Earnings, adjusted for non-recurring costs, came to $1.04 per share. Popular on Variety Revenue came to $29.9 billion, off 1.2% from the year-earlier quarter. Comcast said Peacock generated a profit for the first time since its launch in 2020, with paid subscribers now totaling 48 million, up by two million net additions during the period. The company said it also generated an extra $440 million in revenue due to its World Cup telecasts. Indeed, the World Cup tournament helped power the company’s NBCU operations, which generated a 22.9% increase in revenue, to $10.7 billion in the quarter. U.S. advertising rose 55%, and would have increased 23.5% without World Cup activity, thanks to Peacock and interest in the NBA. Revenue also increased at the company’s studios and theme parks. Revenue was off at the company’s large cable and broadband operations, falling 3.2%. Comcast said it continued to lose subscribers to both cable and broadband, but noted an increase in wireless subscriptions. The company ceded 230,000 subscribers, and now has 47.7 million. title: Whoopi Goldberg Slams Transphobic Critics of Elliot Page’s Casting in ‘The Odyssey’: ‘Then Don’t Go See It… Why Do You Care Who’s Cast in It?’
full_text: Jul 23, 2026 3:07am PT Whoopi Goldberg Slams Transphobic Critics of Elliot Page’s Casting in ‘The Odyssey’: ‘Then Don’t Go See It… Why Do You Care Who’s Cast in It?’ By Naman Ramachandran Plus Icon Naman Ramachandran Latest Venice Title ‘The Difficult Bride’ Taps Films Boutique for World Sales (EXCLUSIVE) 5 minutes ago Isabel Davis Named Director of BFI Filmmaking Fund – Global Bulletin 10 minutes ago Whoopi Goldberg Slams Transphobic Critics of Elliot Page’s Casting in ‘The Odyssey’: ‘Then Don’t Go See It… Why Do You Care Who’s Cast in It?’ 46 minutes ago See All Kristina Bumphrey/Variety/Getty Images Whoopi Goldberg used Tuesday’s episode of “The View” to push back on conservative anger over Elliot Page ‘s casting in Christopher Nolan ‘s “ The Odyssey ,” dismissing a clip of commentator Ben Shapiro calling the choice a mistake. Shapiro called Page’s role an “absolute puzzler” before pointing out that he is “about 5’2 and 105 pounds soaking wet and has the voice of a woman. It doesn’t work.” Related Stories ‘An Icon Is Here’: Juliette Binoche Takes Over Karlovy Vary, Talks Directorial Debut ‘In-I in Motion’ and Explains Why ‘We’re All Creators’ '3 Weeks After' Review: A Bruising, Fiercely Controlled Study of High Schoolers in Moral and Psychological Freefall “A lot of conservatives who see this are going to feel the same way. That’s okay. But I don’t understand. Why go see the movie? If you don’t want to see it, don’t go see it. Why do you care who’s cast in it?” Goldberg said. Popular on Variety Page has a supporting role in the film as Sinon, a cousin of Matt Damon’s Odysseus. The Variety review described Page’s role as “tersely vulnerable.” Goldberg didn’t stop at defending Page. She also went after the word “woke” itself, telling viewers that the label gets thrown at ordinary people just trying to hold down jobs, raise families and get an education, not at anyone pushing an agenda. Critics on the right had first seized on inaccurate rumors that Page would be cast as Achilles, with Elon Musk among those weighing in before the actual casting was known. A similarly hostile response met Lupita Nyong’o’s involvement in the project, rooted in racist objections to her casting. The criticism has had zero effect on box office for “The Odyssey.” Nolan enjoyed the biggest opening of his career with the film, with the global collections now having crossed $300 million. title: Isabel Davis Appointed Director Of The BFI Filmmaking Fund
full_text: Skip to main content Dismiss News Banner Breaking News Venice Film Festival Lineup Announcement — Follow Live Read the full story By Zac Ntim Zac Ntim International Reporter @ZacharyNtim More Stories By Zac Isabel Davis Appointed Director Of The BFI Filmmaking Fund Scottish Ballet & Forest Of Black Partner With Tull Stories To Release Dance Film ‘These Violent Delights’ — First Look Trailer TIFF 2026: ‘Stuffed’ Starring Jodie Comer & Harry Melling Among Discovery Titles View All July 23, 2026 2:30am BFI Isabel Davis has been appointed Director of the BFI Filmmaking Fund. Davis joins the BFI in October 2026 for a five-year fixed term after eight years as Executive Director of Screen Scotland. Before Screen Scotland, Davis served a lengthy term at the BFI, including as the Head of International. Davis takes over from Mia Bays, the outgoing Director of the Filmmaking Fund, who has been in the role since October 2021. Films backed by the BFI during Bays’ tenure include Kneecap, My Father’s Shadow , and the BAFTA-winning animated short Two Black Boys in Paradise . Related Stories News BFI Adds Six Members To Board Of Governors News Edinburgh International Film Festival To Launch UK Film Conference; BFI Head Ben Roberts Set As Inaugural Keynote Speaker Discussing the hire, BFI CEO Ben Roberts said: “Isabel is tenacious when it comes to doing the right thing for UK independent film and filmmakers. This is such a huge job, a cornerstone of UK independent film, and Isabel brings experience, passion, and a national and a global outlook, and a firm commitment to fairness and public service. She will be an asset to the film community and the BFI.” Watch on Deadline Roberts led the recruitment process alongside the BFI’s Deputy CEO Harriet Finney. External panellists were also included during the interview process. They were producers and BFI board members Elizabeth Karlsen and Hakan Kousetta and Nicky Bentham, co-chair of Pact’s Film Producers’ Group. In a statement, Davis said she was “honoured to be stepping into this role at a time when this public funding is critical to building a successful future for UK film and the public value it represents.” “It’s a challenging moment for UK independent film, economically and structurally,” she continued. “But audience appetite for great stories, and for the communal experience of cinema-going, is strikingly resurgent and our filmmaking talent continues to be among the world’s best. Enabling filmmakers to produce work that connects with a broad range of audiences across the UK – through ambition, inventiveness and heart – has to be at the core of our response. Daunting, yes, but exciting too.” Davis ended her statement: “I’ll bring my experience in Scotland to this role, and with it a truly UK-wide perspective on the BFI’s support of independent film. So Scotland hasn’t seen the last of me – nor indeed have Northern Ireland, Wales and the English regions.” Films backed by the BFI Film Fund this year include Shalini Adnani’s Our Share of Sand , which is set for Venice, Theo Rhys’s Stuffed , a Toronto world premiere starring Jodie Comer and Harry Melling, and Clio Barnard’s I See Building Fall Like Lightning , which won the People’s Choice award in Directors’ Fortnight at Cannes. title: Billkin Putthipong To Star In GDH’s ‘Untitled Snooker Project’, Produced By Team Behind ‘Gohan’ & ‘How To Make Millions Before Grandma Dies’
full_text: Skip to main content Dismiss News Banner Breaking News Venice Film Festival Lineup Announcement — Follow Live Read the full story By Liz Shackleton Liz Shackleton pmc-media-editor-2024 More Stories By Liz Edko Films Picks Up World Rights To Yeung Leung Chuen’s Toronto Discovery Title ‘We Are Born Good’ Moebius Entertainment Picks Up World Rights To Fiona Roan Feng-I’s ‘Happily Ever After’ Ahead of Toronto Premiere Na Hong-Jin’s Cannes Competition Title ‘Hope’ Has Strong Opening At Korean Box Office View All July 23, 2026 2:30am L-R: Moderator Opal Panisra, Vanridee Pongsittisak, Billkin Putthipong, Wattanapong ‘Jack’ Wongwan, Baz Poonpiriya Mitchell Jung EXCLUSIVE: Thai studio GDH has announced that Billkin Putthipong Assaratanakul will star in its feature film project, The Untitled Snooker Project , in the role of a professional snooker player. The film, scheduled for theatrical release in late 2027, will be directed by Wattanapong ‘Jack’ Wongwan, whose credits include the hit Netflix series The Believer s. It will be produced by Vanridee Pongsittisak , producer of the global hit Bad Genius ; Oscar-shortlisted box office hit How To Make Millions Before Grandma Dies , and recent hit Gohan , alongside Baz Poonpiriya , producer of Gohan and Not Friends. One of Thailand’s biggest actors and recording artists, Billkin also starred in How To Make Millions Before Grandma Dies , which was shortlisted for Best International Feature at the 97th Academy Awards and grossed around $74M worldwide. Watch on Deadline “We’re excited to bring together an exceptional creative team for this project,” said Thodsapon Thiptinnakorn, Head of Content at GDH. “With Billkin leading the cast and Jack’s distinctive vision as director, we believe this film will deliver a compelling cinematic experience that will resonate with audiences both in Thailand and internationally.” The Untitled Snooker Project is currently in development, with additional casting and story details to be announced at a later date. title: ‘Ted Lasso’ Star Toheeb Jimoh & ‘Snow White’s Dujonna Gift Leading BBC’s ‘Open Water’ Adaptation
full_text: Skip to main content Dismiss News Banner Breaking News Venice Film Festival Lineup Announcement — Follow Live Read the full story By Max Goldbart Max Goldbart International TV Co-Editor @Goldbart1 More Stories By Max CAA Hires Free Focus Talent Chief For UK Creators Division Candle True Stories Bosses On Revisiting Michael Jackson 20 Years After Martin Bashir Doc & Building A ‘20/20’ True Crime Franchise In The UK Lily Allen Feature Doc Coming To Prime Video View All July 23, 2026 2:26am Toheeb Jimoh (left) and Dujonna Gift Emilio Madrid / Marco Sirisi Emmy-nominated Ted Lasso star Toheeb Jimoh and Snow White ’s Dujonna Gift are leading the BBC ’s adaptation of Caleb Azumah Nelson’s debut novel Open Water . Jimoh plays Marcus opposite Gift’s Effie. Marcus works in retail while trying to develop a career as a photographer. Effie is studying dance at university in Dublin. From the moment they meet, they feel an immediate, undeniable connection. But, as Marcus soon learns, Effie is in a relationship with Marcus’ friend Samuel. It’s a boundary that Marcus is unwilling to cross. Jimoh was Emmy nominated for his role as Sam in Apple TV’s smash comedy Ted Lasso. Gift hasn’t acted in TV before but played Maple, Snow White’s best friend, in Disney’s 2025 live action adaptation. Related Stories News BBC Considers Locking Down iPlayer For Viewers Who Refuse To Pay License Fee News BBC Staff In Town Hall Revolt Over 1% Pay Rise, Job Cuts & Romesh Ranganathan Ad: "It's Insulting" Joining Jimoh and Gift are Rhianna Dorris ( Lockwood & Co. ), Lamar Waves ( The Tower ), Nadine Marshall ( Random ), Henrietta Pearl Quartey, Hope Ikpoku Jnr ( Top Boy ), Sekou Diaby ( Animol, Boarders ), Tara Tijani ( Death in Paradise ), Enva Lewis ( How To Have Sex ), Nash Nørgaard ( Death of Ivan Ilyich ), Stevie Odu ( Boiling Point ), Renee Bailey ( Call The Midwife ), Jayden Fowora Knight ( The Nutcracker and the Four Realms), Janina Elkin ( The Queen’s Gambit ), Sherise Blackman ( Bridgerton ), Matthew Dunster ( Coronation Street ), Neve McDonnell ( G’wed ), Javier Bonaparte, Beaux Bryant ( Stolen Dreams Redemption ) and Mo Sesay ( The Diplomat ). Watch on Deadline Unveiled by the BBC two years ago , the show is an adaptation of one of the buzziest books of the decade, with Azumah Nelson set to adapt and direct it himself. His second novel, Small Worlds, was previously optioned by Brock Media. Azumah Nelson said: “The process of bringing Open Water from page to screen has been amazing, and I’ve found some truly outstanding collaborators. We rarely see love stories like this and everyone, from crew to cast, has put their whole heart into this production to make it happen. I can’t wait to share what we’ve been working on.” Jimoh added: “This is a dream project for me. I fell in love with Marcus and Caleb’s story immediately. It’s such a sensitive, character rich, love story, delicately told and full of all the details of the south London I grew up in. It’s such an honour to be a part of this team and I can’t wait to share this one with you all.” Open Water is made by Banijay -backed Mam Tor Productions and B-Side Productions for BBC iPlayer and BBC One. It was commissioned by Lindsay Salt, Director of BBC Drama. Banijay Rights handles international distribution. Azumah Nelson is the lead writer and director and will also serve as executive producer alongside Tally Garner for Mam Tor Productions, Matimba Kabalika for B-Side Productions, and Rebecca Ferguson for the BBC. The series is produced by Karlvyn Dove. Acclaimed composer and musician Cassie Kinoshi will create an original score. The lineup will screen 21 world premieres, six international premieres, one European premiere and 23 regional premiers. One film screening out of competition will have its Bosnian-Herzegovinian premiere. This year, the selection team led by Izeta Građević, Creative Director of the Sarajevo Film Festival , has seen 1,215 films: 210 narrative features, 305 documentaries, and 700 narrative short and student short films. title: Robert Pattinson, Penélope Cruz, Alicia Vikander and Oasis Lead All-Star Venice Line-Up
full_text: × Skip to main content By Scott Roxborough Plus Icon Scott Roxborough Europe Bureau Chief sroxborough Follow Sign Up View All July 23, 2026 2:00am John Malkovich and Sam Rockwell in 'Wild Horse Nine' Courtesy of Searchlight Pictures/Diego Araya Corvalán Venice, it seems, has done it again. The line-up for the 2026 Venice Film Festival , unveiled on Thursday, looks like another star-studded affair featuring plenty of titles with strong award-season potential. Bold-faced names including Robert Pattinson, Penélope Cruz, Alicia Vikander and, according to artistic director Alberto Barbara, Oasis rockers Noel and Liam Gallagher, will be heading to the Lido this year. The 83rd Biennale will also feature new features from festival faves, including Werner Herzog, Hirokazu Koreeda and Martin McDonagh. Related Stories Movies 'Brave New Love' Dives Into Lust, Infidelity, Norms, Motherhood and Shame (Exclusive Clip) TV Netflix Reteams With Yeon Sang-ho for 'Parasyte: Tamiya' Series Irish director McDonagh, whose Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri won Venice’s Golden Lion in 2017 and who was last on the Lido with The Banshees of Inisherin (2022), returns to competition with Wild Horse Nine. John Malkovich and Sam Rockwell star in the black comedy as a pair of veteran CIA officers who, ahead of the 1973 Chilean coup, are sent far from the action to Easter Island. Steve Buscemi, Tom Waits, and Parker Posey co-star. Three Billboards and Banshees both received an Academy Award nomination for Best Picture, with the former won acting Oscars for stars Frances McDormand and Sam Rockwell. McDonagh’s house studio, Searchlight Pictures, will be hoping for a similar run for Wild Horse Nine after its Venice launch next month. French director Florian Zeller, last on the Lido in 2022 with The Son , returns to comeptition with Bunker. The psychological thriller stars Javier Bardem and Penélope Cruz as a couple whose marriage buckles after he, a celebrated architect, accepts a commission to build a bunker for a billionaire. Stephen Graham, Paul Dano and Patrick Schwarzenegger co-star. Sony Pictures Classics has picked up Bunker for North America. FilmNation Entertainment is handling international sales. Legendary German director Werner Herzog, whose documentary Ghost Elephants screened out of competition in Venice last year, arrives in the main selection this year with Bucking Fastard . Herzog reportedly turned down an out-of-competition slot in Cannes for his new feature, which stars siblings Rooney and Kate Mara in their first on-screen collaboration. Orlando Bloom and Domhnall Gleeson co-star. Hanway Films is selling Bucking Fastard worldwide and is still hunting for a domestic distributor. Danish director May el-Toukhy ( Queen of Hearts ) will make her Venice debut in competition with Woman Unknown. The period drama stars Mathilde Arcel as Marie, a young nanny and housemaid who is set to marry her boss, the wealthy older widower, Christian (Carsten Bjørnlund). But Marie’s past — her shameful affair with a German soldier during occupation — threatens to catch up with her. TrustNordisk is handling world sales. In a more surprising move, Cannes darling Nanni Moretti will be returning to the Lido. Moretti’s It Will Happen Tonight , a romantic drama starring Louis Garrel and Jasmine Trinca, will premiere in competition. The Italian auteur, winner of the Palme d’Or for The Son’s Room (2001), was last in Venice with Palombella rossa back in 1989. Playtime and Pan-Européenne are handling world sales for It Will Happen Tonight . Two of South Korea’s most acclaimed directors, Hirokazu Koreeda and Lee Chang-dong, will present their latest features, Look Back and Possible Love , respectively, in competition. Also in competition for the Golden Lion this year are Casey Affleck’s 1970s-set gothic mystery Company starring Nick Nolte and Ben Mendelsohn, and A Bit of Light from Iranian director Ali Asgari. Robert Pattinson will hit the Lido red carpet for the world premiere of Primetime , the feature debut of documentary director Lance Oppenheim ( Some Kind of Heaven ), playing NBC newsman Chris Hansen, whose To Catch a Predator format becomes a media sensation. A24 is releasing the film stateside and handling worldwide sales. Venice has already confirmed that Danny Boyle’s Ink , a biographical drama starring Jack O’Connell, Guy Pearce and Claire Foy, about Rupert Murdoch and his British tabloid empire, will open this year’s event, on Sept. 2. Dio Ride , an Italian period drama from director Giovanni Veronesi starring Pierfrancesco Favino ( Maria , Rush ) will close the fest on Sept. 12, screening out of competition. Maggie Gyllenhaal ( The Lost Daughter, The Bride! ) will head up this year’s Venice competition jury as president, picking the winner of the Golden Lion for best film. George Clooney, a Lido regular, will receive an honorary Golden Lion lifetime achievement award . Among the titles screening out of competition are Place to Be from Hungarian director Kornel Mundruczo ( Pieces of a Woman ) with Ellen Burstyn, Taika Waititi and Pamela Anderson; Let Us Through, Dear Ancestors , the latest from Filipino auteur Lav Diaz ( Season of the Devil ); Paul Schrader’s new film, The Basics of Philosophy ; and the Luc Besson-produced crime thriller Father Joe , from director Barthelemy Grossman starring Kiefer Sutherland and Al Pacino. Announcing the lineup on Thursday, Venice Festival artistic director Alberto Barbara began with the Venice Spotlight sidebar, whose highlights include Conversation With the Sea from Palestinian director Muayad Alayan, and Hombre Al Agua from Mexican star Gael Garcia Bernal. The Spotlight line up looks like a particularly political one this year, with other highlights including Ground Floo r from Hungarian director Gabor Reisz, which Barbara described as a metaphor for the Victor Orban “dictatorship;” and I Matter , from director Alina Serban, the first film from a Roma director to screen in the section. Among the highlights of Venice’s main sidebar, the Orizzonti or Horizons section, include Lovers in the Blue Night , the new film from India’s Anuparna Roy, whose Songs of Forgotten Trees won best director in the section last year; and Too Much Sugar , the new documentary from Procession director Robert Greene. Another new section, the Venice Open Non Fiction sidebar for documentary films, will see the world premiere of several of this year’s most hotly-anticipated docs, including Alex Gibney’s Elon Musk documentary Musk , My Undesirable Friends Part II – Exile , Julia Loktev’s follow-up to her 2025 film tracing Russia’s crack down on independent journalists; and Union Town , from two-time Oscar winner Barbara Kopple ( Harlan County U.S.A. , American Dream ). It remains to be seen if this year’s Biennale will live up to its initial promise. Venice last year included several high-profile premieres, but many — Benny Safdie’s The Smashing Machine , Noah Baumbach’s Jay Kelly , Kathryn Bigelow’s A House of Dynamite — were award season flops. The only 2025 Venice title to go on to score at the Oscars was Guillermo del Toro’s Frankenstein , which picked up 9 Academy Award nominations, winning three, for Best Costume Design, Best Production Design and Best Achievement in Makeup and Hairstyling. Bugonia , from Yorgos Lanthimos, another Venice world premiere, picked up three Oscar nominations but won none. Kaouther Ben Hania’s The Voice of Hind Rajab , which won Venice’s Silver Lion last year, was nominated for Best International Feature but lost out to Joachim Trier’s Sentimental Value . title: Venice Film Festival Lineup: Robert Pattinson’s ‘Primetime,’ Martin McDonagh’s ‘Wild Horse Nine,’ Oasis Reunion Doc and More
full_text: Jul 23, 2026 1:50am PT Venice Film Festival Lineup: Robert Pattinson’s ‘Primetime,’ Martin McDonagh’s ‘Wild Horse Nine,’ Oasis Reunion Doc and More Pattinson and both Liam and Noel Gallagher will be on the Lido to present their projects By Nick Vivarelli , Ellise Shafer Getty Images/Searchlight Pictures/Disney Buzzy new projects from top international directors, including Martin McDonagh, Florian Zeller, Danny Boyle, Werner Herzog, Lance Oppenheim, Nanni Moretti and Hirokazu Kore-eda, are set to bow at the Venice Film Festival . Venice artistic director Alberto Barbera has unveiled his lineup for the upcoming 83rd edition that, somewhat similarly to Cannes earlier this year, is dominated by works from indie filmmakers peppered with plenty of A-list stars despite a dearth of Hollywood studio or streamer-backed fare. Related Stories ‘An Icon Is Here’: Juliette Binoche Takes Over Karlovy Vary, Talks Directorial Debut ‘In-I in Motion’ and Explains Why ‘We’re All Creators’ ‘3 Weeks After’ Director on Tackling ‘Everyday' Peer Violence and Why Conversations About Suicide in Serbia Happen Too Late: ‘We Don’t Notice the Fire’ Which is not to say that the Lido selection lacks lustre or awards consecration potential. It’s a bit more subdued than recent editions, with less Hollywood glitz. But there’s still enough star-wattage and mouth-watering movies to keep film buffs’ pulses racing. Plus, this yearw’s fest will have a dose of rock ‘n’ roll with the world premiere of Oasis reunion doc “Don’t Look Back in Anger,” with British brothers Liam and Noel Gallagher expected to attend. Popular on Variety McDonagh – whose last two features “Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri” and “The Banshees of Inisherin” both premiered in Venice – will be vying for the Golden Lion again with “Wild Horse Nine,” a dark comedy about CIA Agents who are dispatched from Santiago to Easter Island shortly before the 1973 Chilean coup. It stars John Malkovich, Sam Rockwell, Steve Buscemi, Tom Waits and Parker Posey, with Searchlight Pictures distributing. Zeller will be back on the Lido with “Bunker,” his hotly anticipated follow up to “The Son.” It’s an elevated psychological thriller starring Javier Bardem and Penélope Cruz as a real-life couple as a mysterious construction project — a bunker, commissioned by a powerful tech mogul — begins to infiltrate their lives. Paul Dano, Stephen Graham and Patrick Schwarzenegger also star. Sony Pictures Classics has North American and Southeast Asian rights. The previously announced in-competition opener is Boyle’s “Ink” starring Guy Pearce as Rupert Murdoch and Jack O’Connell as Larry Lamb, editor of Murdoch-owned British tabloid “The Sun.” Set in 1969, the film charts how Murdoch and Lamb launched what became Britain’s best-selling tabloid. Claire Foy plays Lamb’s fictional wife, Jules Davies, who also serves a deputy editor of the controversial newspaper. “Ink” will mark Boyle’s first time on the Lido. Netflix recently acquired the U.S. and Latin American rights to the film. Herzog is in competition with “Bucking Fastard” starring sisters Rooney and Kate Mara as twins who “in search of an imaginary land where true love is possible, start digging a tunnel through a mountain range,” according to an official plot description. It marks the first time the Rooney sisters have appeared on screen together. Orlando Bloom and Domhnall Gleeson also star. Herzog’s latest was invited to Cannes earlier this year but he declined after it wasn’t offered a competition slot. U.S director Lance Oppenheim, who directed buzzy HBO documentary series “Ren Faire,” will be in Venice with A24-produced “Primetime” starring Robert Pattinson as a journalist who takes on an underworld of crime and ends up changing television forever. An under-the-radar-title from the U.S. indie trenches set to compete in Venice is Casey Affleck-directed Gothic mystery “Company” starring Nick Nolte, Adelaide Clemens, Ben Mendelsohn, Scoot McNairy, Emily Alyn Lind and Caylee Cowan. Set on a winter night in 1975, “Company” involves parallel tales told by a mysterious woman named Evelyn who arrives uninvited at a holiday gathering. Her stories span across generations. It marks Affleck’s third directorial feature and first since 2019’s “Light of My Life.” The single Hollywood major studio title in the Venice selection is Disney’s “Don’t Look Back in Anger” which follows Oasis’ recent reunion world tour and is touted as featuring the first joint interviews with Noel and Liam in over 25 years. “Don’t Look Back In Anger” has been created by BAFTA and Oscar-nominated writer, producer, and director Steven Knight (“Peaky Blinders”) and is directed by Dylan Southern and Will Lovelace (“Shut Up and Play the Hits,” “Meet Me in the Bathroom”). The Oasis doc – which is playing in Venice as a special event – will open in IMAX venues and cinemas worldwide for a limited theatrical engagement beginning September 11th 2026, before streaming exclusively on Disney+ internationally and on Hulu and Disney+ in the U.S. later this year. Russell Crowe is expected in Venice for “What Love Builds,” his feature-length documentary chronicling performances and tours of his band “Indoor Garden Party” across Europe, the U.S,, UK, and Australia. Luc Besson-penned and produced action thriller “Father Joe” starring Kiefer Sutherland as a man of faith who wages a violent war against 1990s New York City’s criminal underworld is among other out-of-competition titles. Al Pacino plays a powerful mob boss whose empire collides with Father Joe’s crusade. Barthélémy Grossmann (“Arthur: Malediction”) directs. Then there is Paul Schrader’s “The Basics of Philosophy” about a philosophy professor, played by Jack Huston, who has been contending with guilt over a decision from his past when the victim suddenly returns to his life. Daniel Zovatto, Bill Pullman and Shiloh Fernandez and Sofia Boutella also star The drama is said to continue Schrader’s “man in a room” stories that began with his script for Martin Scorsese’s “Taxi Driver.” These have also included Schrader’s more recent features “First Reformed,” “The Card Counter” and “Master Gardener.” Other out-of-competition standouts include Hungarian filmmaker Kornél Mundruczó’s “Place to Be,” starring Ellen Burstyn, Taika Waititi, and Pamela Anderson. The drama which follows the unlikely friendship of an elderly woman and middle-aged man as they travel from Chicago to New York to return a lost racing pigeon home. Alex Gibney will bow his Elon Musk documentary “Musk” that’s described as an epic investigation behind the public persona of one of the world’s most influential and controversial business figures. Barbara Kopple will be in Venice with her new doc “Union Town,” interweaving interconnected labor stories in and around New York City, involving delivery workers, including those working for Amazon, UPS and food-delivery apps. Palestinian director Ahmed Hassouna will be in Venice with doc “Citizen Osama” which portrays a photographer as he attempts to juggle his roles as father and a journalist while capturing the realities of war in Gaza. Kurdish/Norwegian director “Hiding Saddam Hussein” will unveil his feature “No Paradise if You Are Killed by a Woman” which follows a Kurdish-Norwegian female sniper who returns to the battlefield she once escaped, driven by vengeance and by the desperate hope of rescuing her younger sister, still imprisoned by ISIS. Back to the competition. Veteran auteur Nanni Moretti leads a robust Italian contingent with “It Will Happen Tonight” featuring French star Louis Garrel and Italian A-lister Jasmine Trinca (“La Storia,” “Supersex”). Though the plot is being kept under wraps, it’s loosely based on the short story collection “Hungry Heart” (Lev Raev) by Israeli writer Eshkol Nevo. “Happen Tonight” will mark the first time in more than two decades that Moretti – who has had nine movies in competition at Cannes – will launch a movie from the Lido. Chilean-Italian helmer Marco Bechis (“Olympic Garage”) is competing with “Back to Buenos Aires,” his new drama about the crimes of the Argentine dictatorship. “The Echo Chamber,” the latest drama from Andrea Pallaoro starring Alicia Vikander and Luca Marinelli as enraptured lovers and featuring Susan Sarandon, based on an unfinished script by Bernardo Bertolucci, is among other Italian competition standouts. Italy’s younger generation is also repped by Paolo Strippoli’s high-concept thriller “The Spiral” (“L’estranea”) about a problematic mother-daughter relationship starring Jasmine Trinca and Valeria Bruni Tedeschi and dark tragicomedy “Il Fuoco che ti Porti Dentro” by Edoardo De Angelis. The copious French contingent comprises Stéphane Brizé’s “A Good Little Soldier” toplining Italy’s Alba Rohrwacher as a woman navigating a corporate environment where vulnerability is not an asset. Vincent Lindon also stars. Then there is Cédric Kahn’s “15/18” (aka “A Place to Heal”) that is set in a French public hospital’s adolescent psychiatry unit, and Nicolas Pariser’s “A Bit Before Midnight” with Melvil Poupaud as a man examining his life after his father dies. Léonie Simaga, Chiara Mastroianni, and Golshifteh Farahani also star. Asian entries competing include Japanese festival circuit darling Hirokazu Kore-eda’s “Look Back,” a live-action adaptation of a Tatsuki Fujimoto manga story about two girls chasing their dreams of becoming manga artists. It marks Kore-eda’s second premiere this year after his sci-fi fable “Sheep in the Box” which competed at Cannes. There is also beloved Korean helmer Lee Chang-dong’s Netflix movie “Possible Love” that centers on two couples whose vastly different lifestyles create unexpected intersections. The film marks Chang-dong’s first feature since 2018’s “Burning.” And cult Japanese director Shinya Tsukamoto’s “Mr. Nelson, Did You Kill People?” starring U.S. stage actor Rodney Hicks and Geoffrey Rush in a portrait of Vietnam veteran Allen Nelson. Japanese filmmaker Sabu’s detective comedy “Arrested Memory,” starring Taiwan’s Ethan Juan, is screening out-of-competition. Danish-Egyptian director May el-Toukhy who made a splash with 2019 Sundance audience award-winner “Queen of Hearts” will bow post-WWII drama “Woman, Unknown” about a former housekeeper named Marie who while preparing to celebrate her engagement to the patriarch of the house, is haunted by secrets of her past. El-Toukhy, who has also directed episodes of Netflix’s “The Crown,” is the single female director in the 20-title competition. Berlin-based Ilya Khrzhanovsky will unveil the new entry in his monumental DAU project, which he spent over two and a half years finishing. “DAU. Natasha” and “DAU. Degeneration” previously premiered at the 2020 Berlinale. Khrzhanovsky renounced his Russian citizenship and has been an outspoken critic of Putin’s regime. But he also faced allegations about the way he treated women on the set of “DAU. Natasha.” Iranian director who was in Venice Horizons last year with dark satire “Divine Comedy,” in back – in competition this time – with his new feature “A Bit of Light.” Opening the Horizons section dedicated to more cutting-edge works is Alina Marazzi’s “La Ragazza Con La Leica” (“The Girl With the Leica”) about war photographer Gerda Taro, the first woman photojournalist killed in war who was also Robert Capa’s muse As previously announced, Maggie Gyllenhaal will preside over the jury. Gyllenhaal will also be presenting an out-of-competition short titled “Flesh Impact” with Ellen Burstyn, Dakota Johnson, Peter Sarsgaard, and Sepideh Moafi. She will be joined on the jury by Hong Kong filmmaking giant Johnnie To, Tunisian director Kaouther Ben Hania, Afghan filmmaker Shahrbanoo Sadat, French director and screenwriter Xavier Giannoli, “The Brutalist” composer Daniel Blumberg and U.S.-based Italian film academic Francesco Casetti. George Clooney and Ellen Burstyn will also be honored with lifetime achievement awards. The 83rd Venice Film Festival runs from Sept. 2 to 12. See the full lineup below. COMPETITION “Ink,” Danny Boyle (opening film) “Company,” Casey Affleck “Kami Nour” (“A Bit of Light”), Ali Asgari “Ritorno a Buenos Aires,” Marco Bechis “Un Bon Petit Soldat” (“A Good Little Soldier”), Stéphane Brizé “Il Fuoco Che Ti Porti Dentro,” Edoardo De Angelis “Kvinde Ukendt” (“Woman Unknown”), May El-Toukhy “Bucking Fastard,” Werner Herzog “15/18 (A Place to Heal),” Cèdric Kahn “Dau,” Ilya Khrzhanovsky “Look Back,” Hirokazu Kore-eda “Ga-Neung-Han Sa-Rang” (“Possible Love”), Lee Chang-Dong “Wild Horse Nine,” Martin McDonagh “It Will Happen Tonight,” Nanni Moretti “Primetime,” title: Netflix Steps Back Into ‘Parasyte’ Universe With ‘Tamiya’
full_text: Skip to main content Dismiss News Banner Breaking News Venice Film Festival Lineup Announcement — Follow Live Read the full story By Jesse Whittock Jesse Whittock International TV Co-Editor @WhittockTweets More Stories By Jesse Canadian Entertainment Biz Veterans Team To Launch Paper Route Studios Alon Shtruzman & Scott Einbinder Launch Microdrama Studio New Short Media BuzzFeed Studios & MuVPix Set 100-Title Microdrama Production Pact, Starting With ‘Romeo & Juliet, Reimagined’ View All July 23, 2026 1:47am 'Parasyte: Tamiya' Netflix Netflix has returned to the Parasyte manga universe that began in 2024 with Parasyte: The Grey . A new live-action series, Parasyte: Tamiya will star Shioli Kutsuna ( Sanctuary , Deadpool 2 ) and will stream in 2027. Parasyte: The Grey creator Yeon Sang-ho is returning, having developed Tamiya and co-written the scripts with Ryu Yong-jae. Wowpoint , the Grey producer, is also back, with Kensaku Kakimoto ( Glass Heart ) and Kazuhiro Nakagawa ( Double , Bullet Train Explosion ) directing. Parasyte: The Grey spent four weeks at the top of Netflix’s Global Top 10 Non-English list in 2024, and reached number one in 34 countries. Tamiya will develop the story further. Related Stories Casting Taylor Zakhar Perez, Owen Thiele, Nasim Pedrad & Supriya Ganesh Among Final Additions To Netflix's '13 Going On 30' Reboot News Rory Scovel Sets New Netflix Comedy Special 'Show Must Go On' In the original manga, Ryoko Tamiya is a parasite who takes over the body of a high school teacher. Initially brutal and indiscriminate, she gradually learns from her human surroundings — and upon witnessing a parasite and human sharing a body, begins to wonder whether coexistence might offer her species a better chance of survival than conflict. Watch on Deadline Parasyte: Tamiya will tell Ryoko’s story from the inside: the original human, the parasite who inhabits her and the evolving creature caught between two worlds. Kutsuna plays the character, and said it was “the most challenging role I have ever played in my acting career.” “Ryoko Tamiya is a truly captivating villain in the original manga,” said Yeon. “Her struggles over the nature of her own existence are what elevate the original ‘Parasyte’ from a simple hero-based manga aimed at kids to something deeper. She’s a fascinating character, which drove me to want to tell the story of Parasyte through her perspective. “I wanted to convey her perpetual struggle over the need for parasites to coexist with humans and the issue of their survival, and how she suffers setbacks and learns over time. I think the anguish she experiences is the essence of Parasyte .” Parasyte is a major IP in Japan, having previously been adapted as a film series, an anime and a Korean drama. “This series, which is currently being edited, brings new things to the table compared to past entries in terms of story, cinematography, action, production, you name it,” said Yeon. “It firmly reflects the vision of both directors and is made with great respect for the original manga.” Reid Scott Leaving ‘Law & Order’, James Badge Dale To Succeed Him In Season 26 ‘Stewie’ Voice Cast Set With Thompson, Bayer & Others In ‘Family Guy’ Spinoff Company Seeks 3-Day Hearing As Judge Mulls Injunction; EU OKs Merger Sony Circling Ridley Scott’s ‘Treasure Island’ Starring Hugh Jackman Read More About: Comments

title: Daniel Wu on ‘Cold War 1994,’ ‘Twilight of the Warriors’ and Coming Home to Hong Kong: ‘The Craft Still Challenges Me’
full_text: Jul 23, 2026 1:41am PT Daniel Wu on ‘Cold War 1994,’ ‘Twilight of the Warriors’ and Coming Home to Hong Kong: ‘The Craft Still Challenges Me’ By Naman Ramachandran Plus Icon Naman Ramachandran Latest Venice Title ‘The Difficult Bride’ Taps Films Boutique for World Sales (EXCLUSIVE) 13 minutes ago Isabel Davis Named Director of BFI Filmmaking Fund – Global Bulletin 18 minutes ago Whoopi Goldberg Slams Transphobic Critics of Elliot Page’s Casting in ‘The Odyssey’: ‘Then Don’t Go See It… Why Do You Care Who’s Cast in It?’ 54 minutes ago See All ©Crystal Li Daniel Wu received the Best from the East Award at the New York Asian Film Festival , capping a stretch that has put him back at the center of Hong Kong’s two biggest franchises: “ Cold War 1994 ” and the “Twilight of the Warriors” series. “Cold War 1994” is the third installment in the “Cold War” series, a prequel written and directed by Longman Leung and produced by Bill Kong and Ivy Ho, set in the run-up to Hong Kong’s 1997 handover. The first two films, released in 2012 and 2016, were the highest-grossing Hong Kong productions of their respective years. In the new film, Wu plays Peter Choi opposite Terrance Lau’s M.B. Lee, a character previously portrayed as a full-blown villain by Chang Kuo-chu in “Cold War 2.” Related Stories Myanmar-Set ‘Fruit Gathering’ Wins at Karlovy Vary: ‘Harrowing Drama of Obsession and Queer Desire’ Harvey Keitel Shooting 'No Bias,' Written by Wife Daphna Kastner, Calls Out ‘Danger’ of AI When Mentioning Michael Caine AI-Narrated ‘The Odyssey’ Audiobook Wu tells Variety he never consciously stepped away from Hong Kong film. The pandemic simply thinned out a schedule that had settled into a rhythm of one American project and one Asian project a year. When “Cold War” came along, the pull was the production itself. Popular on Variety “It’s going to be a quality production, the scale of it, the script,” Wu says of what drew him back, pointing also to the chance to work alongside three generations of Hong Kong actors, including Lau, whom he singles out as representative of the industry’s new generation. The film folds a police procedural into a much larger political canvas, tracking the British colonial government, the Hong Kong police, a powerful business dynasty and the criminal underworld as the territory changes hands. Wu, who was 22 at the time of the actual handover, says the setting let the production dig into a period of corruption and upheaval he lived through but never fully understood until now. Playing a younger version of a character already established as an antagonist gave Wu a different kind of challenge than he’s used to. He calls the role the most morally ambiguous he’s taken on, since it required tracing how a man who becomes an “evil mastermind” gets there gradually, through a series of compromised choices, rather than starting out that way. Returning to a Hong Kong set after several years away felt less like a job and more like a reunion, Wu says. He recalls arriving for his costume fitting and recognizing nearly everyone in the room, a small-industry familiarity he doesn’t get on American productions, where he’s lucky to know one or two people on a given crew. Sitting down on set with his rice box for lunch, eating char siu fan alongside people he’d worked with for decades, brought back what he calls a genuinely warm feeling. He credits Hong Kong crews with a particular work ethic: “efficient, fast, and no bullshit,” a directness he links to a broader Hong Kong attitude of simply getting things done. Wu spent much of his “Cold War 1994” press tour in Hong Kong wearing a hat that, it turns out, was hiding a new haircut for his role in “Twilight of the Warriors: The Final Chapter,” the sequel to director Soi Cheang’s “ Twilight of the Warriors: Walled In .” That 2024 film, adapted from Yu Yi’s novel “City of Darkness” and the manhua of the same name by Andy Seto, became Hong Kong’s second-biggest commercial hit ever and swept the Hong Kong Film Awards. Where “Walled In” was set in the 1980s, the new installment moves the story to 1993, the year Kowloon’s Walled City was demolished. Wu, who studied the Walled City’s architecture in school, calls the setting a rare chance to build a mainstream action franchise around a genuinely singular piece of Hong Kong urban history. He sees both projects as part of a broader shift in how Hong Kong cinema treats its own past, using the 1990s, a decade he says filmmakers “glossed over” while living through it, as material for reflection rather than backdrop. That shift, Wu says, is happening at a moment when Hong Kong productions can draw on newer digital tools while still leaning on the practical, in-camera stunt work the industry built its reputation on, recalling a time when performers “threw themselves off the top of a building and just did it for real in camera.” He pushes back on the long-running narrative that Hong Kong film is dying, arguing instead that it’s in something closer to a renaissance, even with a fraction of the annual output it had in the 1980s and ’90s. “There’s a lot of interesting films being made, whether big scale or small scale, a lot of great performances, a lot of great scripts being written,” he says. The kinds of roles Wu is offered in Hong Kong have shifted too, from the younger leading-man parts that defined much of his career to more mature, fatherly characters, a change he credits partly to age and partly to the character work he’s done in the U.S., where his credits have included the AMC martial-arts series “Into the Badlands,” HBO’s “Westworld,” the film “Tomb Raider” and the animated series “American Born Chinese.” He says that kind of opportunity has thinned out generally, arguing Hollywood has moved past a period of genuinely interesting roles for Asian American actors and back toward “crumbs” or stereotypical parts. He’s more pointed about the ceiling he still sees in the industry, where he says opportunities for Asian American actors, and especially Asian American males, remain narrow. Moving between the two industries has left Wu with what he describes as an unusual sense of identity, neither fully of one world nor the other, but both at once. He notes that his experience differs from most Asian American peers, many of whom have never topped a call sheet, while his own career has run the opposite path: character roles in the U.S., leading roles in Asia. He also points to a structural difference between the two systems: Asian productions tend to run through a single director’s vision, while larger American productions are often shaped by committee, with multiple producers weighing in, leaving him unsure at times whose direction to follow. Looking back over 25 years, Wu points to his collaboration with director Derek Yee, spanning six films including “One Nite in Mongkok,” “Drink-Drank-Drunk,” “Protégé” and “Triple Tap,” as the turning point that pulled him away from the romantic leads he was initially cast in and toward the darker, more complex characters he prefers. He likens the partnership to Robert De Niro’s with Martin Scorsese, a shorthand so complete that he’d commit to a Yee project without reading the script. “That put my career in a direction that I wanted it to go,” he says. Wu is now developing two scripts for the American market and one for the mainland Chinese market, a deliberate move toward more proactive control over his own projects, with an eye toward eventually returning to directing, nearly two decades after his 2007 feature debut, “Heavenly Kings.” Whatever comes next, he says the appeal hasn’t changed. Whether it’s a script’s demands or the actors around him, every project still teaches him something new. “I would say the craft, the craft still challenges me,” Wu says.
